---
title: "Chapter 4: Placer-Au prospectivity (northern Sierra)"
subtitle: "Two-population (Tertiary deep-gravel + Quaternary modern-channel) calibrated probability raster at 250 m on the canonical California placer province"
---

[← back to portfolio map](index.html)

## What this is

A 250 m calibrated probability raster for placer-Au prospectivity across the
northern Sierra deep-gravel belt, built as two per-population classifiers
(Tertiary buried paleo-channels, Quaternary modern-channel alluvium) fused
into a single GeoTIFF that the [goldbug](https://johnsondevco.com/goldbug/)
live tool samples per claim. The geological setting is Lindgren 1911 (USGS
Professional Paper 73) at canonical resolution: hydraulic-mined deep gravels
from Malakoff Diggins south through Forest Hill, plus the modern Yuba, Bear,
and American river placers below them.

Placer-Au modeling sits about a decade behind lode in the published
literature. The lode side has stacked supervised pipelines with spatial-block
cross-validation, leakage audits, and external blind tests as standard
practice. The placer side mostly does not, because the data is sparse (point
occurrences with inconsistent `dep_type` strings), the positives concentrate
in a few well-known districts, and the geomorphic chain from lode source to
placer trap involves transport mechanics that don't show up in a static
feature stack. The only production-grade transferable placer model in the
published record is the USGS Alaska watershed scorer used in the Cross,
Frost, and Karl assessments, and even that is a knowledge-driven weighted
index rather than a supervised classifier. This chapter ports the Alaska
scorer to the northern Sierra at 250 m, then layers a supervised pass on
top that catches the v2-era leakage shortcut and measures the residual leak
in the v3 build. The deliverable feeds Chapter 3 (goldbug); the sibling Cape
Nome property in Chapter 1 (Bear Cub) is different geology and a different
audience but shares the population-level reasoning.

## What got built

**Phase 1 knowledge-driven index.** I ported the USGS Alaska watershed scorer
to the northern Sierra at 250 m grid resolution and added Sierra-specific
features for deep-gravel geometry (hillside benches above modern drainage,
not channel bottoms). Eleven features combine into a p1/p99-clipped
min-max-normalized weighted sum: `distance_to_lode_m`,
`paleochannel_likelihood` (a composite of NHDPlus HR REM, local relief, and
geomorphon GMI), `hydraulic_pit_proximity_m`, `tertiary_terrace_likelihood`
(TPI-high ∧ slope-low ∧ not-Qa geometric mean), `catchment_au_hawkes` (Hawkes
1976 dual-decay catchment aggregation of NGDB + NURE stream-sediment Au), and
six others. Seven historic hydraulic-mining districts (Malakoff Diggins,
North San Juan, Dutch Flat, You Bet, Forest Hill, Iowa Hill, Michigan Bluff)
define the validation gate: each must land in the top decile of the score,
and none appears in the training label set. The Phase 1 model gets 7 of 7. A
held-out Lindgren PP73 secondary set of 13 Tertiary-named districts
(Smartsville, Camptonville, Alleghany, Damascus, and nine others) lands 11
of 13 in the top quintile at median decile 0.

**Phase 2 supervised, per population.** I trained one classifier stack per
geomorphic placer population. Three base learners run under 20 km
spatial-block cross-validation with per-fold Hawkes catchment-geochem
recompute: Random Forest, LightGBM, XGBoost. A logistic-regression
meta-learner combines the spatial-block out-of-fold predictions. Isotonic
calibration via `CalibratedClassifierCV` produces the final probability
scale. The two populations train on disjoint feature stacks (17 features for
Tertiary, 22 for Quaternary) and fuse per-cell via `np.maximum` into the
deliverable raster. Quaternary uses the Kiryo et al. 2017 non-negative PU
loss (`nnPU`) instead of the Mordelet-Vert PU bagging the Tertiary side
uses, because the 0.054% Quaternary positive rate sits inside nnPU's design
range. Anchor districts and Lindgren secondary cells are masked out of
training (label-side and pseudo-negative-side) so the validation gates stay
real held-out tests.

**Leakage-resistant feature stack.** v2 had `hydraulic_pit_proximity_m` as a
raw distance feature, and the v2 Random Forest used it as a label-inverse:
positive labels are pit polygon centroids, so the distance-to-pit feature at
zero IS the label. v3 sets the feature to NaN within 1 km of any pit polygon
so the model cannot trivially identify positives by zero-distance lookup.
Distance-to-lode and distance-to-fault features switched from v2's hard 30
km caps to Gaussian falloffs (σ = 12 km and 3 km, respectively), so
districts like Forest Hill at 12 km from the nearest mapped lode source get
a non-zero score instead of v2's hard zero. MRDS placer records
reclassified per CGS 2010 PTYPE plus 500 m pit-proximity: 136 records that
v2 assigned to Quaternary moved to Tertiary because they sit on Tertiary
auriferous-gravel mapped units within pit-proximity range. Final v3 positive
counts: 292 Tertiary cells and 437 Quaternary cells across an 805,294-cell
AOI.

**Held-out validation.** The Lindgren PP73 secondary set is the diagnostic
that distinguishes v2 (leakage) from v3 (signal). Eleven Tertiary-named
secondary districts land at 81.8% in the top quintile of the v3 Tertiary
calibrated raster (median decile 1), all 500 m de-duped from any training
pit. v2 put 8 of those 13 in the zero bin. Anchor districts hit 7 of 7 in
the top decile of the v3 raster, with capture@1% of 71.4% (5 of 7 anchors in
the top 1% of cells, enrichment 71x) and capture@5% of 85.7%. Calibration
ECE measured pre-isotonic at 0.0002 and post-isotonic at 0.0002; the input
stack arrives well-calibrated and isotonic doesn't have room to move it.

![The v3 Tertiary calibrated probability raster, displayed as percentile rank across the AOI because the calibrated probability has a long zero tail that linear color stretching would wash out. Cyan outlines are the 158 Orlando 2016 hydraulic-mine pit polygons that supplied the training labels. Red dots mark the seven anchor districts, none of which appear in the training labels; all seven land in the top decile. The hot zone traces the Mother Lode trend through the western half of the AOI; the eastern half toward the Sierra crest is mostly zero-probability black.](data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/v3_calibrated_tertiary_map.png)

**Quaternary v3 in training.** The Quaternary classifier is finishing its
stacking out-of-fold stage as this chapter is being written. Numbers ship in
the next render. The Tertiary results above are final for v3.

## What we learned

**v1: a fixed weighted index is the diagnostic, not the model.** Phase 1
puts 7 of 7 anchors in the top decile, but the eleven weights are
geologist-set rather than learned from labels. Phase 1 is the held-out gate
v2 and v3 have to clear; it isn't the deliverable. It also fails to
specialize. Phase 1 runs a single Tertiary-tuned scorer across both
populations because all seven training anchors are Tertiary, but Quaternary
modern-channel placers need stream power, catchment Hawkes, and
`is_quaternary_alluvium` weighted differently. v2 and v3 fix this with
per-population classifiers.

**v2: the headline number was a leakage artifact.** v2 Tertiary RF averaged
0.995 across 14 spatial blocks with standard deviation 0.008. The model was
reading `hydraulic_pit_proximity_m` as a label-inverse: pit polygon
centroids ARE the positive labels, and a Random Forest with deep trees and
no shrinkage on small leaves trivially identifies the cells at zero
distance. The stacking meta-learner pulled the headline back to 0.961
because LightGBM resisted the shortcut, but the Lindgren held-out result
told the real story. Five of the 13 Tertiary secondary districts landed in
the d1 cluster matching the anchors; eight landed in the d9 zero bin. That
is not the gate passing. It is the model declining to rank the held-out
cells above the floor because the shortcut feature has nothing to anchor
their probability to.

![Of the 13 Tertiary-named Lindgren PP73 secondary districts I hand-geocoded, the 500 m dedup buffer against trained positives drops Alleghany and Indian Hill, leaving 11 that v2 and v3 both score. The v2 calibrated raster put 7 of those 11 in the zero bin (d9) and 4 in d1, with nothing in the top decile. The v3 raster moves all 11 out of d9: 2 land in d0 (Smartsville and Cherokee), 5 in d1, 3 in d2, and one outlier (Lake City) at d7. Median decile shifts from d9 to d1, top quintile from 5 of 13 to 7 of 11. The 500 m buffer plus the per-population feature stack do what they were supposed to do: the held-out Tertiary names are no longer landing on the v2 raster's zero pile.](data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/v3_vs_v2_lindgren_decile_histogram.png)

**v3 closes the leak but doesn't fully erase it.** Buffering
`hydraulic_pit_proximity_m` to NaN within 1 km of any pit prevents the
trivial zero-distance shortcut, but SHAP TreeExplainer puts
`hydraulic_pit_proximity_m_buffered` at #1 in the top-5 feature ranking, 2.7x
the mean absolute SHAP value of the second feature (gravity).

![SHAP TreeExplainer ranks `hydraulic_pit_proximity_m_buffered` as the dominant feature for the v3 Tertiary stack, 2.7x the mean absolute SHAP value of the next feature (`gravity`). The 1 km buffer pushes the trivial zero-distance shortcut out of reach, but the feature still encodes Tertiary belt membership; the ablation below measures how much of that lead is real geological signal vs residual shortcut the buffer admits.](data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/v3_shap_top5_tertiary.png)

I ran an ablation to measure the residual leak: retrain Tertiary RF and XGB
without the feature entirely. RF dropped 10.5 percentage points (0.749 →
0.644) and XGB dropped 11.8 pp (0.826 → 0.708). About ten points of the v3
base AUCs are the buffered feature doing legitimate Tertiary-belt encoding
(cells in the broader hydraulic-mining belt have a real geological reason
to score higher) plus whatever residual shortcut the 1 km buffer still
admits.

![I dropped `hydraulic_pit_proximity_m_buffered` from the v3 Tertiary feature stack and retrained the RF and XGB base learners on the same 20 spatial-block folds. RF AUC fell 10.5 percentage points (0.749 to 0.644); XGB fell 11.8 pp (0.826 to 0.708). About ten points of each model's baseline AUC was the buffered feature doing real Tertiary-belt encoding plus whatever residual shortcut the 1 km buffer admits; the remaining 0.64 to 0.71 AUC is what the other fifteen features carry on their own.](data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/v3_ablation_no_pit_proximity.png)

The Lindgren secondaries pass at 81.8% top quintile because they live in
the Tertiary belt by definition, so the belt-membership component of the
feature predicts them correctly even after the trivial zero-distance lookup
is blocked. v3.5 widens the buffer to 2 km to partition those ten points
between real signal and residual leak.

## What this doesn't prove

- **The hot cells contain extractable gold.** Hydraulic-pit polygons mark
  where 1850s-1884 miners chose to wash hillsides, not where commercial pay
  exists today. The calibrated probability is "this cell looks like a
  Tertiary-deep-gravel or Quaternary-modern-channel placer setting," not
  "you will find gold here at depth X with thickness Y."
- **The model transfers off the AOI.** A Calaveras County transfer test
  (same Mother Lode trend, different watersheds) is queued. Expect a drop
  comparable to the Klamath-region transfer on the lode side (Mother Lode →
  Klamath went from AUC 0.85 to 0.58). Within-AOI generalization is the
  story so far.
- **The Tertiary stacking out-of-fold AUC of 0.970 is the headline number.**
  The ablation says about ten points of the base AUCs come from
  `hydraulic_pit_proximity_m_buffered`, partly real Tertiary-belt encoding
  and partly residual shortcut. The leak-free number is closer to
  0.85-0.90, which still beats the projected post-leakage band of 0.75-0.85
  the v3 redesign aimed at.
- **The Phase 1 anchor gate is a sufficient external test.** Lindgren PP73
  secondaries are the better test, because they were never seen by any
  classifier or scorer and they are 500 m de-duped from any training pit
  polygon. v3 passes Lindgren at 81.8% top quintile; v2 did not.

## Where to look

The deliverable rasters live at
`data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/prospectivity_placer_placer_tertiary_250m_calibrated_{3310,4326}.tif`
and the matching Quaternary pair when the v3 Quaternary pipeline finishes.
[goldbug](https://johnsondevco.com/goldbug/) ingests the fused raster via
its zonal-mean machinery: per-claim mean + max + count + has-data flag
against the calibrated probability, weighted by the claim polygon
footprint. No API contract beyond the GeoTIFF.

For the full data + model walkthrough (input layer maps, the v2-to-v3
feature redesign, the ablation measurement chart, the leakage audit
traces), see the
[data + model overview notebook](notebooks/northern_sierra_placer/data_overview.html).
That notebook consolidates what previously lived across an internal
data-exploration walkthrough and five v3-phase review notebooks.

[← back to portfolio map](index.html)
